speaker: interviewee
text: Good old Oregon rain.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've had a lot of rain, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. This year.

speaker: interviewer
text: -- Aigner. Tape three with Leslie Aigner. OK. We were going to-- you were-- I was asking you if you witnessed some other beatings. And we'll take it from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was constant and always around that-- around us, the beating. If you don't-- didn't push the wagon or didn't do-- or didn't run fast enough, they-- whenever they ordered us to go into the-- to the bed or the-- if they caught us on the way-- or they just, on a whim, the kapos were brutal. They, they, they-- some-- I saw some people's arm broken with a stick. And I-- the only thing I-- like a little-- I don't know how should I explain it-- I just would try to hide from them and get in the middle of the group so I don't be on the edge where they-- where they-- and I tried to do the work, because that was the only thing, or, or the thing what they told me to do to get away. But even then, I didn't, didn't always got away. But luckily I wasn't injured behind-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] able to recover from it. And many nights, when-- even though it was said you can't leave the barrack, I saw people hanging on the barbed wire, electrified in the morning. We heard their scream when they grab the wire at night. Walked out and just grabbed the wire and-- they couldn't take it anymore. I don't know what made me going on. I didn't grasp the intensity of the situation or I just-- like a little animal, I was try to do-- I make another day. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I, I-- in that 10 months in concentration camp, I saw more dead people that I can count, and I, I, I have probably-- never will see in my life, I hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wanted-- you were telling how you cleaned out-- how you took the barrels out at night. Could you talk about the sanitary--

speaker: interviewee
text: The cleanings?

speaker: interviewer
text: The cleanings and the sanitary conditions themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, it was fair. I say fair. I took a shower when I got into Auschwitz, and that was the last shower I took for the next 11 months, or 10 and 1/2, 11 months. But in Auschwitz, we had a barrack, a so-called wash barrack, where they had basins with running water. So we were able to wash our shirts and clothes and put on that and let it dry on us in the warmer days. But we weren't able to wash it in the winter months, because it would have-- we would have froze. And that-- the latrine was, again, another barrack with rows of cement pads with holes on them. And hundreds of people were sitting down at the same time. As a matter of fact, a small incident happened with me in a latrine. I still-- I was wearing my glasses, and a little gypsy boy came by and grabbed my glasses where I-- where I was sitting. And that was the last time I had glasses in the camp. I don't know why he needed it or what he did with it, but he grabbed my glasses from my face and run away with it. Never caught up with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could you see after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm shortsighted, but, you know, I can read close, and-- which we didn't. We didn't have chance. But basically I can see even now but-- without glasses-- but I need my glasses. Since age 6, I wear them like grown to my face.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's one thing I'm not sure if you told me. Maybe I missed it. But after Dr. Epstein had sent you out of the hospital and then you were bleeding, you never really explained, I don't think, how your foot healed. How did you manage? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it, it was still sore. It was still open sore. I put bandage on. I, I got some rags to put on. And I just managed with the sore foot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went out to Landsberg with the sore foot. And it healed, you know, in December. It was December, and by January, it was fairly healed. And so I tried to hide my limping.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also said that when you arrived in Auschwitz and-- a couple of times you were face to face with Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: I been in front of Dr. Mengele once in a railroad track and twice in the encampment in the 3 and 1/2 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that? Can you talk about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first time, I didn't know. I just went to the direction where they sent me. The second and third time, we were jittery. We were scared. We were just-- which way we'll be sent?

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you dressed--

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew it. We knew it, what we going through, because by then we knew that the one who is hardly walks or old or, or slip through in the first selection-- but it didn't slip through the second and the third. And so when it came to the fourth selection of Dr. Mengele as a transport out of Auschwitz, I know I won't make it. And that's why I traded myself out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you dressed when you saw Mengele? Or when he saw you? Maybe that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to take off the top. And that's about it. And the pants was on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So unlike the women, who were completely naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, only one time they ask us to push down the pants. But no, we, we-- generally we were half naked, just half naked when the inspection came. They told us to take off our coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm sorry. I just wanted to get some of these details in that I think are important to have on the tape. So you traded yourself out. Do you know what happened to the man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, ironically, I never even ask his name. We just traded and I hope he made it, because actually I left Auschwitz in early December or so, approximately. I don't know dates. And Auschwitz was liberated mid-January. But then again from Auschwitz, as I heard now, they were transferred, walking out of Auschwitz in midwinter. And I don't know if I would've made it with my foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of train-- what kind of transport was it you were placed on, and where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived to a town named Landsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what kind of train were you on? Were you on an open train? Were you on a closed train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was was a cattle car, closed cattle car where I was. And we arrived, I don't know even how many days we traveled, a couple of days, probably. Arrived to town, and we heard the, the name. It was Landsberg. And we marched through the town of Landsberg between the SS guards. And one thing still sticks in my mind that I-- one of the older person said, this is the prison of Landsberg where Hitler wrote At the time, it didn't meant nothing to me because I didn't know nothing about at that age. I don't know. Maybe I was ignorant, but I didn't know about it. And it stuck to my-- that Hitler wrote he said that. And it was in Landsberg where we started working on, on some installation of airplane repair place, steel-reinforced concrete. It seemed to me we were building a hangar. But as I later learned, it was an underground factory. But the part I was working on, it was a hangar. We were ordered on a mini railroad train, and we were taken to this building site. We were working, carrying the, the cement, the material, and, and the, the, the sand and-- in these little railroad tracks. We were pushing them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And one thing sticks to my mind. This one time, as we were pushing this loaded railroad car, one of the fellow fell in between the, the steel reinforcement. Must've broken some. He was screaming and yelling. The SS guard never let that man stop the pulling of the cement. And his screaming is still in my ear today. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] These are the things which sticks in my mind, because it was so outrageously brutal and horrifying that I can't forget it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as I said, we were working, and I was losing weight. The food was less and less, and the war was coming to an end. We heard the Allies' artillery in the distance and the bombing. And a group which worked at night, many, many times they were stopped at the wall because the Allies' bombing came. And they shut off the lights. They were just sitting around in the cold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were working in two different-- two shifts, a day and night. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from December till mid-March or so, we were building. Then they stopped this building of this factory. I don't think they ever finished it. And my condition was such that, you know, it, it took twice as many men to do one man's job. And we were just leaning against those carts to push-- to be able to push it on the railroad tracks. And Landsberg and Kaufering is very close by. We were ordered from the Landsberg camp to march into Kaufering camp. And they said this is a hospital camp, and we will be taken care of there. They called it hospital camp. It was a A-frame barracks on the ground, and a little walkway cut into the middle. And on two side, the people of were sleeping and laying down shoulder to shoulder. We had a little wood stove, a barrel-- a little half-- a little barrel for heat. And I contracted in this Kaufering camp-- I don't know the number. They have numbers for it-- contracted typhus. In this camp, Landsberg and Kaufering, the cleaning condition was almost zilch, almost nothing. We got hold of a bucket of water occasionally, and we washed ourselves, several of us, with a piece of rag. And that was our-- extent of our cleaning.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all had lice, and we contracted-- I contracted typhus, so I was separated, quarantined, and I had to go in one of these A-frame barracks, who everybody who had fever-- high fever and typhus-- typhoid? Typhus? Typhus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that week or 10 days I was in there, I don't know how long, high fever, very little food or water. Whatever they brought in, the other fellows, and distributed among us. About a week or 10 days later, when I came to, I was-- ample room around me. There was-- when I was taken in there, it-- we were shoulder to shoulder. There were ample room around me. The rest of them were carried out. As I say, as I walked out, I took off my trousers, and both of my shin was covered with lice. It was like an ant hill on my shin. And I when I swept it off, my skin went with it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yet one nice thing happened in, in-- with us in Kaufering that-- I don't know if the American or the International Red Cross was let in for inspection. As I said, this was the very end of World War II. The, the heavy artillery was-- we were hearing it all day, all night by then. The Red Cross was let in, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they distributed some blankets and some food. Still remember getting a can of Borden's milk. I cherished it for days. It, it really helped me survive. One thing I forgot, and I always say that specially in the schools when I'm talking about the Holocaust for children. When we were ordered to march from Landsberg camp to the Kaufering camp, which was 5 kilometers or so, it took us most of the day to walk. As we walked through the town, townspeople from behind the fences and from the windows threw food between our lines. And this was the first time I-- I-- we realized that they endangered their own safety, because we were marching between the guards, and we were grabbing whatever they threw in. But they disappeared behind the windows right away. They were throwing food, seeing our condition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: German people. And so I-- so there was some feeling people, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me, if one were to look at a map, where Landsberg and Kaufering would be in Germany, so one--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. If you have Munchen in one point, Dachau is about 15 kilometers off of Munchen. And from Dachau down south is Landsberg and Kaufering area. About 50 or 60 kilometers from Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was, as I said, the last leg of the war. By then-- this time it was April, by the time I came out of that barrack. Barely able to walk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it still didn't end. We-- I didn't got liberated in, in Kaufering. The Nazis had a-- another saga for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were ordered-- many of the people were ordered and they were marching. I was ordered to go march out to the railroad station and back into the cattle cars. But this time they had open wagons and regular cattle cars. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the only difference was that the railroad tracks on one track. The Nazis were retreating with the heavy artillery, ammunition, and all their hardware. On another track, the concentration camp inmates in the train, train rode. The Nazis were hoping maybe because we are there, they will not be bombed. It would have been a short trip, but it took us almost three days to get to Dachau. What had happened, we got hit by air raid. They bombed the-- both of the tracks They machine-gunned our train. And in the train where I was sitting, this time it wasn't so loaded. We were all squatting down. Both of the fellow beside me got hit by machine gunfire. And I just dazed. By then, the guards were gone. We opened the railroad car and stumbled out of the car and walked into the woods nearby and hid there. I remember going out on the fields and dug up some potatoes for food. But they came back the next morning or a day later with dogs, gathered us together, and ordered us back to the railroad cars. They never removed the bodies. This is how we arrived to Dachau. It was past the-- or around 20th or 22nd of, of-- I know the date, because I know the liberation date. Approximately on the 20th or 22nd of April, 1945, this is how we arrived to Dachau. And they call this train the death train, because more dead bodies arrived to Dachau on this train than live ones. And I was ordered into the camp, walked through the gate into the barracks, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I don't know if I was there a week, five days or maybe 10 days, at the most. Five days, a week. And we were liberated April 29 by the Seventh Army, as I learned later. April 29, 1945. It was a glorious day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe liberation day for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to describe. We were crying from happiness. I still intend to cry. And I, I really couldn't go out to greet the liberators. I was just leaning against a barrack, because I was about 75, 80 pound, a walking skeleton. If I would have gone away from the building, I would have fallen and would've been trampled. I just was waiting and watching everybody's happiness there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Seventh Army. United States Seventh Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Dachau, in the back in Dachau where they have the-- where the ovens are located, which they didn't use to the way they did in Auschwitz, do you remember any of that at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. I, I was so out of it. My strength had left me. I, I crawled and I hardly was able to walk by then. And I don't know why they kept me, really. Just luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: There-- Were you given candy, food? How did you-- how-- were you nourished?

speaker: interviewee
text: The liberation was great. We were getting all the food. As a matter of fact, we got too much food in the first three days. I got hold of a can of corned beef, and I was eating it by the spoonful. Actually after three days, I threw most of it away. It spoiled on me. This was April. It's wasn't too warm, but it was already. After three days, I had to throw it away. But I didn't need it after three days. Some of the fellows who couldn't hold back and ate that rich food never made it. They're weakened condition couldn't. Then the American medics came in. They gave us all the DDT in our pants and shirts to get rid of the lice. And I was taken to the Seventh Army field hospital and I was there for three or so-- about three weeks to get my strength back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they were transferred, going back to each direction of the world-- France, Holland, Poland, Czechoslovakia, Hungary. I got on one of the transfer to Czechoslovakia. But before I go further, in the liberation day, I saw-- witnessed this, that some of the Nazi guards who were left, because the command-- high command already left the camp early-- some of the Nazi guards hid in the garbage dump, which was a deep-- a tractor was able to go in there and had a cover on it. Hid in there and when we were liberated, the concentration camp inmates seek them out, started to stone them and beat them. And the Seventh Army detail came in and held a gun against us not to beat them to death. Some of them were already, but we were held at bay not to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you something about in the notes that you gave me something-- unless I missed it-- but you have in there something about suicide that you didn't talk about. Was that meaning where the men went to the-- you saw the men who went to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to the barbed wire. Yes. They couldn't take it any longer and they grabbed the electrified wire to get out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in the field hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three weeks. And I have papers as I follow through Prague and Bratislava, Czechoslovakia going toward home. Because the, the American troop carriers, trucks, they dropped us off in Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went through a disinfection again. They gave us food. And they directed us, if you want to go at that time, the trains were going. And it looks like nobody needed tickets because we were traveling on top of the train and on the tail end of the train. It was so loaded. So I went to Bratislava, and I have a piece of paper. I went through again disinfection in Bratislava. And this is how I arrived on the top of the train to Nove Zamky, to my hometown. And I spent, in Nove Zamky-- I don't know if a day or two. There was very little organization in this little town. And I think I slept in the old synagogue one night, and I didn't find nobody. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Ironically, I went just a-- couple of examples, to Miami to the Holocaust reunion of 50 years. And I find somebody from Nove Zamky over there. It was interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone you'd known as child or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's actually her sister was my classmate. And she was the-- this was the older sister, and they survived. We recog-- in this gathering in Miami, there were tables-- Czechoslovakians, Hungarians. And we went there and started asking questions. And one lady knew these people from Nove Zamky. And she told me that she's in Sarasota. That's where we stayed with my wife's cousin. That-- that's where we went to the Miami conference. And we called them, and we met up with her. It was interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to find out about your mother? When were you absolutely sure about your mother and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father, when we-- when I returned-- well I-- then I left Nove Zamky. And I went off to Budapest. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went up to the paper where have my sister worked, and some fellow who we-- I knew came out after work from the paper. And I waited at the gate till I recognized somebody. And he told me my sister escaped from the encampment and went into Budapest and put herself up as bombed out from the countryside and worked as a maid, as a gentile maid for some family through the rest of the few months of the war. This is how she survived. And when I heard that, I-- he gave me some point where I can find her. And then I find my father. And my father survived by escaping to the Russian side, on the Russian front, when the Nazis were heading into Russia. And then they-- the offensive started, and they were pushing back the Nazis. My father hid, and behind the Russian lines came back to Budapest. And that's when he got my letter what I wrote in Auschwitz. I forgot to mention. On second week I was in Auschwitz, they ordered us to write a letter to somebody who is home. Because the Budapest ghetto basically was there. So I didn't write. Didn't have nobody else. But in Csepel, in the town where we lived the last time, we had a gentile friend. And I addressed this postcard to this gentile friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't-- I wasn't able to write in German, because it has to be written in German. So one of my fellow-- I ask write me this letter. Say, well, what you want to write? We couldn't write anything but that we are OK, and we are well. So I tried to hid between the sentences something. So I wrote-- or asked him to write, 'Dear Uncle [? Anthel, ?] let my father and my sister know that I'm OK, and I am with my friends,' letting them know that I'm not with my mother and my sister, candidly. I don't know if they ever understood it, but this is what it was all about, the letter, the postcard. Thank you.
